name: Conrad Hill Mine near Lexington
short_description: Historic feldspar and mica mine in the Piedmont region offering rockhounders access to pegmatite minerals and quartz. A moderate difficulty site with good potential for small gem finds.
description:
The Conrad Hill Mine near Lexington is a historic feldspar and mica mining site located in Davidson County within North Carolina's Piedmont geological zone. This location has been worked intermittently since the early 1900s, with several periods of commercial feldspar extraction alongside small-scale mica recovery. Today, the site remains accessible for rockhounding with the potential to find well-formed quartz crystals, feldspar specimens, and mica books in the exposed pegmatite walls and surrounding spoil piles.

The geological setting here is characteristic of the North Carolina Piedmont, where Precambrian metamorphic rocks host pegmatite dikes rich in feldspathic minerals. These pegmatites formed during the Alleghenian orogeny when continental collision created deep-seated magmatic conditions ideal for crystal growth. The weathered outcrops and mine tailings create excellent collecting opportunities, particularly in spring and fall when water exposure is moderate.

Visitors should expect rolling terrain with scattered rock exposures and old mine workings. The site requires moderate hiking ability to navigate the uneven ground, abandoned pits, and rough terrain. Wear sturdy boots and be cautious around loose rubble. The location is popular with local rockhounds and offers the chance to encounter large feldspar crystals and high-quality mica sheets alongside quartz specimens.
history: The Conrad Hill Mine operated as a commercial feldspar producer in the early-to-mid 20th century, extracting feldspathic pegmatites common throughout the North Carolina Piedmont. These pegmatites formed during Precambrian time and were later enriched by hydrothermal processes during the Alleghenian mountain-building event, creating the ideal mineralogical conditions for quartz, feldspar, and mica crystal development.
tips: Search the exposed pegmatite walls and old spoil piles for quartz crystals and feldspar; the mine's north-facing slope often yields the best specimens after rainfall. Bring a small hammer and chisel to carefully extract crystals from the host rock, and look for silvery mica sheets in the weathered pegmatite. The site is most productive in spring (April-May) when seasonal moisture enhances mineral visibility and rock conditions are stable. Focus on areas where white feldspar and clear quartz are visibly exposed in the old workings.
terrain_notes: Rolling Piedmont terrain with elevation around 800-850 feet. Moderate slope with dense deciduous and mixed pine forest cover. Uneven rocky ground with numerous loose boulders, old mine pits, and exposed pegmatite outcrops. Approximately 0.5 miles hiking required from parking area. Footing is difficult due to loose rock, exposed tree roots, and uneven mine spoil.
written_directions: From Lexington, take NC 8 north for approximately 4 miles toward Denton. Turn east onto Old Providence Church Road and travel 2 miles. Continue on the unpaved mining road for 0.5 miles until you reach the old mine workings. Park in the designated pullout area near the main pegmatite exposure. GPS coordinates (35.78481, -80.16534) mark the historic mine site entrance.
road_conditions: Unpaved mining road in fair condition; high-clearance passenger vehicles recommended. Washboard surface, passable year-round but muddy during heavy rain.
parking_notes: Small unpaved pullout near mine entrance with space for 3-4 vehicles. No formal parking facilities; park off the mining road to allow access.
collection_rules: Personal rockhounding use only. Collect small specimens for personal collection. Do not disturb or remove large blocks. Respect private property boundaries; stay within the active mine area. No commercial collection or sale. Pack out all trash. Be cautious around abandoned mine workings and unstable ground.
rules:
Personal use collection only
No commercial activity
Do not damage structures or equipment
Be cautious around abandoned mine pits
Pack out all trash
Respect property boundaries
nearest_services: Lexington, NC - approximately 4 miles west. Full services including gas stations, restaurants, and grocery stores available.
accessibility_notes: Moderate terrain requiring good footing and hiking ability. Uneven rocky ground with loose boulders and old mine pits makes wheelchair and stroller access impossible. Sturdy hiking boots essential. Not suitable for visitors with limited mobility or balance issues. Children should be closely supervised around mine pits and unstable areas.

faq.question: What gemstones can I find at Conrad Hill Mine near Lexington?
faq.answer: Conrad Hill Mine is known for quartz crystals, feldspar specimens, and mica sheets. The pegmatite formation here produces well-formed white and clear quartz, milky feldspar, and excellent quality mica books. Small to medium crystals are most common, with occasional larger specimens found in the exposed pegmatite walls.

faq.question: Is there a fee to rockhound at Conrad Hill Mine in Davidson County?
faq.answer: No, there is no admission fee to collect at Conrad Hill Mine. This is a public rockhounding site that allows personal use collection at no charge. You simply need to park at the mine entrance and hike to the collecting areas. However, commercial collection and resale are not permitted.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to collect at the Conrad Hill Mine near Lexington, NC?
faq.answer: No permit is required for personal rockhounding at Conrad Hill Mine. This historic mining site is open to public collecting without special permission or documentation. However, always respect property boundaries and stay within the designated mine area to avoid trespassing.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound at Conrad Hill Mine in North Carolina?
faq.answer: April through May and September through October are the ideal rockhounding seasons here. Spring offers moderate moisture that makes crystal identification easier, while fall provides comfortable temperatures and stable mine conditions. Summer heat and winter cold are less favorable for this site.

faq.question: What tools and equipment do I need to collect at Conrad Hill Mine?
faq.answer: Bring a small hammer, chisel, and pry bar to extract crystals from pegmatite. Wear sturdy hiking boots with ankle support due to uneven, rocky terrain. A small backpack, hand lens for examining specimens, safety glasses, and plenty of water are essential. Consider bringing a headlamp if exploring shadowed pegmatite faces.

faq.question: Is Conrad Hill Mine suitable for beginner rockhounders?
faq.answer: Conrad Hill Mine is better suited for intermediate rockhounders rather than complete beginners. The terrain is moderately challenging with loose rock and abandoned mine pits, and it requires careful specimen extraction techniques. Beginners would benefit from going with an experienced rockhound or taking a local mineral club field trip to this site.

meta_title: Conrad Hill Mine Rockhounding - North Carolina
meta_description: Find quartz, feldspar, and mica at Conrad Hill Mine near Lexington, NC. Public rockhounding site open year-round with no fee. Learn collecting tips and directions.
